coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal pollution is one of the primary challenges of water pollution, and the fabrication of highly effective, green and non-toxic adsorbents for heavy metals is urgently required on the basis of environmental and sustainable development strategies. Here, we report a novel fluorescent wood (FW) with effective adsorption ability (maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of 98.14 mg/g for hexavalent chromium [Cr(VI)]), good fluorescence properties (absolute quantum yield of 12.8%), non-cytotoxicity (cell viability of >90%) and high detection sensitivity and selectivity for Cr(VI). The FW was formed using a process involving delignification, infiltration with carbon dots, and free-radical polymerization with acrylic acid. Mechanistic analysis confirmed that the reconstructed 3D porous structure of the FW provided many effective sorption sites, such as amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. This improved the adsorption ability and stabilized the fluorescence signal, which enhanced the detection ability. These factors give the novel FW considerable potential for use in the removal of Cr(VI) ions from wastewater.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent wood with non-cytotoxicity for effective adsorption and sensitive detection of heavy metals

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal pollution is one of the primary challenges of water pollution, and the fabrication of highly effective, green and non-toxic adsorbents for heavy metals is urgently required on the basis of environmental and sustainable development strategies. Here, we report a novel fluorescent wood (FW) with effective adsorption ability (maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of 98.14 mg/g for hexavalent chromium [Cr(VI)]), good fluorescence properties (absolute quantum yield of 12.8%), non-cytotoxicity (cell viability of >90%) and high detection sensitivity and selectivity for Cr(VI). The FW was formed using a process involving delignification, infiltration with carbon dots, and free-radical polymerization with acrylic acid. Mechanistic analysis confirmed that the reconstructed 3D porous structure of the FW provided many effective sorption sites, such as amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. This improved the adsorption ability and stabilized the fluorescence signal, which enhanced the detection ability. These factors give the novel FW considerable potential for use in the removal of Cr(VI) ions from wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution is one of the leading problems of water pollution. Because of its persistence, bioaccumulation and toxicity, heavy metal pollution is a serious threat to human survival and the natural environment. Thus, it has been drawing the attention of researchers (Tofighy and Mohammadi, 2011; Zhou et al., 2016). Various methods have been established to remove heavy metals from wastewater, such as chemical precipitation (Hu et al., 2005), membrane filtration (Li et al., 2017a; Taha et al., 2012), photocatalytic degradation (Deng et al., 2019; Zheng et al., 2017) and adsorption (Zhao et al., 2016). Among these methods, physical or chemical adsorption is considered the most promising. Activated carbon, clay minerals, metal oxides and synthetic polymeric adsorbents are traditional adsorbents used for wastewater treatment, but their applications are limited due to their high cost, low adsorption capacity and difficult separation, among others (Novais et al., 2018; Esmaeili et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2016). In accordance with environmental and sustainable development strategies, highly effective, green and non-toxic adsorbents should be explored for use in heavy metal removal.

Wood is a common natural biomass material, and its extensive use is consistent with ecological, environmental and sustainable requirements (Cai et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2020). Recently, wood-derived nanomaterials, such as cellulose nanofibers, have been attracting attention in relation to the design of advanced heavy metal adsorbents because of their unique physical and chemical properties (Shen et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2017). However, in the preliminary preparation of cellulose nanofibers, multiple steps are required to reconstruct the nanocrystalline structure. This preparation method is inefficient and destroys the original regular structure of cellulose. Recent reports show a new direction: direct engineering of natural wood (NW) can preserve its natural structure during delignification to obtain a highly aligned cellulose skeleton (Zhu et al., 2016; Bi et al., 2018). Based on this method, several novel hydrogels have been prepared, such as a strong and ionically conductive wood hydrogel (Kong et al., 2018); a robust and flexible composite hydrogel for muscle-like sensors (Chen et al., 2020); and a wood-inspired morphologically tuneable hydrogel for high-performance, flexible, all-solid-state supercapacitors (Zhao et al., 2020). These examples demonstrate the wide potential of directly utilising NW for synthesising high-performance materials.

Carbon dots (CDs) are fluorescent nanomaterials that contain active functional groups on the surface. They have excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility and non-toxicity (Li et al., 2017b, 2019; Gong et al., 2018). Many reports indicate that they can be used for fluorescent detection of heavy metals (Yarur et al., 2019; He et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019). However, similar to many other 0D nanomaterials, CDs are easily aggregated and therefore unsuitable for storage. CDs can be immobilised in a suitable matrix to overcome this problem, and NW is a promising matrix due to its natural hydrophilicity and porous structure, which will help immobilize CDs.

Given the abovementioned background, this work aims to develop a novel fluorescent wood (FW) based on NW and CDs that would be simultaneously used as an effective adsorbent for the removal of heavy metals and as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of heavy metals. The novel FW’s chemical structure, physical structure and hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) adsorption and detection characteristics were characterized, and the cytotoxicity was evaluated in NIH/3T3 cells. Then, mechanistic analysis was performed for the high-sensitivity detection and effective adsorption of Cr(VI).

Basswood was purchased from Jizhi Company of China. Ethylenediamine (EDA), citric acid (CA), acrylic acid (AA), N′,N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA) and potassium persulfate (PPS) were purchased from Aladdin Company.

NW specimens were obtained from a main stem without compressing the basswood. These NW specimens were added to a Soxhlet extractor containing a mixture of benzene and alcohol at a ratio of 2:1 by volume, and the mixture was allowed to react for 48 h. Then, these specimens were washed with distilled water to remove the benzene and alcohol. Subsequently, these specimens were heated in a mixture of an aqueous sodium chlorite solution (2 wt%) and acetate buffer (2 mol/L, pH 4.6) at 90 ℃ for 48 h, followed by rinsing with distilled water. Then, they were placed in an aqueous solution of H2O2 (5 mol/L) and heated at 90 ℃ for 1 h. Finally, these specimens were thoroughly rinsed with distilled water, suspended in ethanol for three days and labeled DW for further use.

CDs have traditionally been fabricated via hydrothermal methods, and a similar approach was used here (Zhu et al., 2013). First, EDA (17 μL) and CA (0.1 g) were dissolved in distilled water (5 mL). The solution was poured into a polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon)-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 200 ℃ for 6 h. The reaction solution was then cooled to 20 ℃, followed by dialysis, to obtain CDs.

The DW specimens were separately immersed in a CDs solution (6 mL) with concentrations of 0.0002 wt%, 0.0004 wt%, 0.0006 wt%, 0.0008 wt%, 0.0010 wt%, 0.0012 wt%, 0.0014 wt% or 0.0016 wt%. The DW specimens were sealed in glass bottles and subjected to a vacuum using a pump to allow the CDs solution to penetrate. The vacuum was held for 30 min and then released. This vacuum impregnation was repeated five times. The DW specimens containing CDs were then immersed in absolute ethanol for one day. Then, a solution of AA (5 g), PPS (0.005 g), NaOH (11 mL, 5 mol/L) and MBA (0.06 g) was mixed well at 0 ℃ in a nitrogen atmosphere. The DW specimens containing CDs were soaked in this reaction solution (20 mL) and then subjected to a vacuum using a pump to allow the reaction solution to penetrate. The vacuum was held for 30 min and then released. This vacuum impregnation was repeated five times. Then, these specimens were heated to 60 ℃ for 12 h to form FW. The prepared FW was further immersed in distilled water for two days to remove unreacted impurities, and the distilled water was repeatedly changed. Meanwhile, the FW specimens with different contents of CDs were named FW-1, FW-2, FW-3, FW-4, FW-5, FW-6, FW-7 and FW-8.

Adsorption of Cr(VI) ions was measured in batch experiments using the FW, DW and NW. In these experiments, ~0.10 g of each specimen was thoroughly mixed with a 100 mL solution of Cr(VI) ions. The effect of pH was studied in the pH range of 1–6 at 20 ℃ and constant concentration of Cr(VI) ions (100 mg/L). The effect of temperature was determined at 20 °C, 30 °C, 40 °C and 50 °C at pH 2 under magnetic stirring for two days. The sorption isotherm was collected with various concentrations of Cr(VI) ions (from 20 to 120 mg/L) at pH 2 and 20 °C under magnetic stirring for two days. The adsorption kinetics was studied with various contact times (from 0 to 240 min) at pH 2, 20 °C and constant concentration of Cr(VI) ions (100 mg/L). The concentrations were measured using an Optima 8300 inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA). All adsorption data were the average of triplicate measurements with <5% relative errors.

SEM images were obtained using an S-4800 scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). XRD analysis was performed using a D8 Advance X-ray diffraction spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). An IN 10™ FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to characterize the prepared FW. UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured using a UV-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and a FLS9800 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK).

Fig. 1 shows the fabrication of schematic diagram. NW and CDs were the two key components. Via delignification, the lignin in NW was mostly removed to release the tight connections within the natural cellulose skeleton. The delignification expanded the transport channel to expose the sorption sites (Jia et al., 2018; Li et al., 2016), and DW was prepared. Meanwhile, CDs with many amino groups were fabricated using a hydrothermal reaction. Subsequently, through the first vacuum impregnation, CDs were attached to DW (Zhang et al., 2015; Ma et al., 2020). Then, via the second vacuum impregnation, the transport channel of DW was filled with AA and MBA solutions. On these bases, FW was prepared using free-radical polymerization.

Fig. 2 shows the microstructures of NW, DW and FW. NW had a 3D porous structure (Fig. 2a–d). After the delignification process, most of the lignin was removed, while the inherent porous structure in DW was expanded (Fig. 2e–h). After two rounds of vacuum impregnation and free-radical polymerization, the reconstructed 3D porous structure in FW was formed (Fig. 2i–l).

Fig. 3a shows the XRD spectra of NW, DW and FW. All three specimens displayed two peaks (22.2° and 16.2°) that were attributed to the two crystal structures of cellulose I ((101) and (002)) (Dong et al., 2016; Moniruzzaman and Ono, 2013). The typical peaks associated with cellulose II were not observed, indicating that the delignification and free-radical polymerization did not alter the crystalline structure of the natural cellulose skeleton. Furthermore, with the use of the Segal method, the crystalline index (CrI) was used to describe the change in the crystal structure (Chen et al., 2020). The values of CrI were 0.23, 0.43 and 0.60 for NW, DW and FW, respectively. The CrI of DW was higher than that of NW because of the removal of the lignin matrix. FW had the largest CrI, which may be due to the reconstruction of the 3D porous structure.

Fig. 3b shows the FTIR spectra of NW, DW, and FW. For NW, the broad band at 3330 cm−1 was attributed to an OH group (Yaddanapudi et al., 2017), the two bands at 1595 and 1502 cm−1 were assigned to the aromatic ring in the lignin (Celino et al., 2014), and the band at 1056 cm−1 was associated with the carbon-oxygen stretching vibrations and ring vibrations in the cellulose species (Celino et al., 2014). In the case of DW, the two bands at 1595 and 1502 cm−1 were not observed, confirming that the lignin was mostly removed during the delignification process. For FW, one new peak emerged at 1664 cm−1; it was associated with –CO–NH– (Wu et al., 2019). This finding confirmed that the free-radical polymerization using MBA as the linkage reagent was carried out. The peak at 1056 cm−1 could not be clearly observed, which further indicated that AA was grafted on the natural cellulose skeleton during the free-radical polymerization.

In the UV–vis spectrum of FW (Fig. 3c), the band at 342 nm, associated with the CDs, was observed clearly (Omidi et al., 2017). In the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3c), FW exhibited one excitation peak at 368 nm and one emission band at 448 nm. As shown in Fig. 3d, the emission wavelengths varied, with excitation wavelengths from 308 to 428 nm. These results further confirmed that the CDs were fixed into FW.

A series of FW specimens with varying quantities of CDs exhibited blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 3e). With an increase in the quantity of CDs, the fluorescent brightness increased and then decreased. Of the FW specimens, FW-5 demonstrated the strongest fluorescent brightness, with an absolute quantum yield (AQY) of 12.8%. The AQY measurement is described in Supplementary Material (Note S1). Cross sections of FW-5 are shown in Fig. 3f and g. FW-5 had the same brightness in the interior as that on the surface, indicating a uniform distribution of CDs from the exterior to its interior. A high-resolution TEM image further confirmed the uniform distribution of CDs within FW-5 (Fig. S1).
The effects of pH, temperature, initial concentration and contact time on adsorption capacity were investigated to examine the Cr(VI) adsorption properties of FW.

The effect of pH on the adsorption capacity of FW-5 is shown in Fig. 4a. In the pH range of 1–6, the adsorption capacity rose with the increase in pH value from 1 to 2 before decreasing as the pH value further increased to 6. The variation in the adsorption capacity with RH was correlated with the electrostatic attraction between positively charged FW-5 and Cr (VI) ions, especially at pH <2. Non-ionic H2CrO4 could not be adsorbed, which caused the decrease in adsorption capacity. Consequently, the pH value of 2 was set as the optimal pH.

The Cr(VI) sorption of FW at various temperatures is shown in Fig. 4b. In the temperature range of 20–50 ℃, the sorption capacity increased with the temperature. This variation indicated that this adsorption process was an endothermic reaction. High temperatures could enhance the adsorption of FW; however, there was no significant increase in its adsorption capacity within this temperature range. Considering practical application, an appropriate temperature (20 ℃) was used for further study.

The effect of the initial Cr(VI) concentration on the adsorption capacity of FW toward Cr(VI) ions was studied to determine the maximum adsorption capacity of FW. As observed in Fig. 4c, an increase in the initial Cr(VI) concentration led to an increase in the adsorption capacities of FW-5, DW and NW, but the adsorption capacity of FW-5 was the highest. For further analysis of the sorption isotherm of FW-5, two well-known models (Langmuir and Freundlich) were used (Fig. 4e and f). The fitting parameters, including the correlation coefficient (R 2), were also summarized. A higher R 2 value indicated that the sorption isotherm was better described by the Langmuir model (Sahraei and Ghaemy, 2017; Yi et al., 2018). The maximum theoretical adsorption capacity was calculated using the this model as 98.14 mg/g (Naushad et al., 2016; Jiang et al., 2015). More importantly, this numerical value is one of the highest adsorption capacities measured for Cr(VI) ions in wood-derived adsorbents to date ( Table 1). This high adsorption capacity demonstrated that FW had good adsorption performance for Cr(VI) ions (Fig. S2).

The effects of contact time on the sorption of FW-5, DW and NW for Cr(VI) ions were determined to study the kinetic sorption (Fig. 4d). Findings showed FW-5 adsorbed more Cr(VI) ions than DW and NW in the same period of time, which qualitatively indicated that the adsorption speed of FW-5 was the highest. Two well-known models (pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order) were used to further analyze the adsorption kinetics of FW-5 (Fig. 4g and h) (Wen et al., 2020). The fitting parameters, including the correlation coefficient R 2), were also summarized. A higher value of R 2 confirmed that the adsorption data were better represented by the pseudo-second-order model. This result indicated that the chemical adsorption might be the rate-limiting step, which agreed with previous works (Panchan et al., 2018; Tian et al., 2018).

Furthermore, the regeneration and reusability of FW-5 were evaluated. The adsorption/desorption cycles were repeated four times, with a NaOH solution (1 mol/L) selected as the eluent. After four cycles, the adsorption capacity of FW-5 remained 82% above the initial value (Fig. S3). This indicated that FW had good regeneration and reusability (Han et al., 2019); thus, it is a potential adsorbent in wastewater treatment.

For the evaluation of detection selectivity, the effects of 16 metal ions (Ba2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Sr2+, Ca2+, Li+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Cr6+, Co2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, K+ and Na+) on the fluorescence of FW were investigated ( Fig. 5a and b). The results indicated that the Cr(VI) ions led to an evident quenching of the fluorescence intensity of FW-5, whereas the other metal ions had a negligible quenching effect on the fluorescence. This may be explained by the fact that Cr(VI) ions had the strongest electron-capturing ability; thus, more electrons could be transferred from the CDs to Cr(VI) ions, resulting in the evident quenching (Yang et al., 2013).

The anti-interference performance of the FW/Cr(VI) system was studied, and the coexisting metal ions (Ba2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Sr2+, Ca2+, Li+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, K+ and Na+) led to an insignificant change in the fluorescence ( Fig. 6a and b). Furthermore, the anti-interference performance in the presence of all these coexisting metal ions is shown in Fig. S4. Among the metal ions tested, the Cr(VI) ions were the most responsive species, which illustrated that the prepared FW had an effective anti-interference ability (Geng et al., 2015). The AQY of FW-5 was measured after various storage times (Fig. 6c). The results confirmed that the fluorescence of FW remained stable after long-term storage (Cheng et al., 2019).

As shown in the inset of Fig. 6d, after exposure to various concentrations of Cr(VI), an evident fluorescence change in FW-5 from blue to black was observed under a 365 nm UV lamp. The AQY of FW-5 varied linearly with the Cr(VI) concentration, from 5 to 200 mg/L. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 3.7 mg/L using the three-standard-deviations (LOD = 3σ/S, where S is the slope of the calibration curve and σ is the standard deviation of ten blank measurements) (Rao et al., 2017; Aziz et al., 2019). Moreover, the detection performance (linear range, LOD) of FW-5 was compared with those in previous works (Table S1). Results showed that FW had a wider linear range and higher LOD, which may be due to its high adsorption capacity. More importantly, FW exhibited both adsorption and detection functions.

FW-5 was applied to analyze Cr(VI) ions in practical samples (tap water and lake water) to demonstrate the applicability of FW. However, there may not have been sufficient Cr(VI) ions in the practical samples, so Cr(VI) standard solutions were added. Recoveries between 96.42% and 104.32% with relative standard deviations of < 4.10% were observed (Table S2). Therefore, FW effectively detected the Cr(VI) ions in the practical samples.

The cytotoxicity of FW was evaluated in NIH/3T3 cells using a CCK-8 assay (Supplementary Material Note S2) (Zou et al., 2018; Li et al., 2015). As shown in Fig. 7a, the cell viability of NW and FW-5 was ~90%, with a concentration range of 15.6–1000 μg/mL, which suggested that FW was not toxic to the NIH/3T3 cells and that no cytotoxic compounds were released from FW. In particular, the cell viability of FW-5 was >120% at a concentration of 1000 μg/mL. Furthermore, live–dead staining was performed using fluorescence microscopy (Bjorkblom et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018). Fig. 7b shows the live cells (stained blue) and dead cells (stained red). Throughout the figure, many blue dots but only a few red dots were observed, suggesting that most of the cells were alive. These results confirmed that FW was non-toxic.

FTIR analyses were performed to examine the adsorption and detection mechanism. Fig. 8a shows the FTIR spectra of FW-5 before and after the adsorption of Cr(VI); two clear reductions in the stretching vibration peak of N–H and O–H at 3339 cm−1 and the stretching vibration peak of –COO− at 1562 cm−1 were observed (Zulfiqar et al., 2014; Lee et al., 1997). Furthermore, one peak at 902 cm−1, which was attributed to Cr, was found (Ren et al., 2016). These results indicated the −OH, −NH2, and COOH groups were effective sorption sites for Cr(VI) ions.

XPS analysis was further applied to characterize the changes in the functional groups before and after adsorption. The XPS survey of FW-5 (Fig. 8b) revealed that FW-5 had carbon (284.8 eV), nitrogen (399.7 eV), sodium (497.0 eV) and oxygen (531.3 eV). Moreover, compared with the XPS survey before sorption, two new peaks at 580 and 589 eV appeared after sorption which were associated with Cr2p3/2 and Cr2p1/2, indicating that Cr(VI) ions were adsorbed on FW-5. In the Cr2p spectrum of FW-5 after adsorption (Fig. 8c), the Cr2p3/2 and Cr2p1/2 regions could be divided into four component bands. The intensities at 576.6 and 586.2 eV indicated the Cr(III) ions, and the intensities at 579.8 and 588.9 eV were characteristic of Cr(VI) ions. These results indicated some Cr(VI) ions were partially reduced to Cr(III) ions (Jiang et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2014). In addition, Fig. 8d shows the O1s spectra. The O1s peak could be split into C˭O (532.8 eV) and C–O (531.6 eV) components (Wang et al., 2018). These two components showed clear changes before and after Cr(VI) sorption, indicating the contribution of carbonyl group toward Cr(VI) sorption (Zhou et al., 2018). Moreover, Fig. 8e shows the N1s spectra of FW-5. No new bands appeared after sorption, indicating that the interaction between N and Cr was not complexation.

On the basis of the foregoing discussion, the adsorption and detection mechanisms are displayed in Fig. 9. First, Cr(VI) ions were concentrated on FW’s surface, driven by the electrostatic force between Cr(VI) ions and protonated amine groups of FW. Then, owing to the reconstructed 3D porous structure of FW, many transport channels were generated for Cr(VI) ions, thus accelerating the diffusion of Cr(VI) ions from the surface to the interior of FW. In the Cr(VI) diffusion process, a large number of Cr(VI) ions were adsorbed on FW, some Cr(VI) ions were partially reduced to Cr(III) ions and the hydroxyl groups were oxidized at the same time. Then, a portion of these reduced Cr(III) ions were attached to the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of FW through electrostatic attraction. During the “adsorption-coupled reduction” process, FW showed effective adsorption ability for Cr(VI) ions. Moreover, due to its reconstructed 3D porous structure, FW prevented the aggregation of CDs, thus stabilising their fluorescence signal. Once the Cr(VI) ions were attached to the amine groups of CDs, the fluorescence quenching became evident. Therefore, FW had sensitive detection ability for Cr(VI) ions.

In this work, a novel FW was developed for the effective adsorption and sensitive detection of Cr(VI) ions. The FW showed a high maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of 98.14 mg/g for Cr(VI) and selective sensing of Cr(VI), with a linear range of 5–200 mg/L and an LOD of 3.7 mg/L. Moreover, FW showed good biocompatibility and non-toxicity. All these characteristics position FW as a highly effective, green and non-toxic adsorbent for Cr(VI) ions.

Hanmeng Yuan: Investigation. Tingting Ren: Software. Qiuyan Luo: Investigation. Yifeng Huang: Software. Yong Huang: Supervision. Dong Xu: Resources. Xin Guo: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Validation, Supervision. Xianjun Li: Supervision. Yiqiang Wu: Supervision.
